definition: Chi (or Qi) is the vital life force energy that flows through all living things. In traditional Chinese medicine and martial arts like Qigong and Tai Chi, cultivating and balancing Chi is essential for physical health, mental clarity, and spiritual well-being.

Qigong (Chi Kung) is a holistic system of coordinated body posture and movement, breathing, and meditation used for the purposes of health, spirituality, and martial arts training. It involves cultivating and balancing the body's vital energy (Chi).

Tai Chi (Taijiquan) is an internal Chinese martial art practiced for both its defense training and its health benefits. Though originally conceived as a martial art, it is also typically practiced for a variety of other personal reasons: competitive wrestling in the format of pushing hands (tui shou), demonstration competitions, and achieving greater longevity.

Wushu, which translates to 'martial art', is a modern exhibition and full-contact sport created in the People's Republic of China. It was developed to standardize the practice of traditional Chinese martial arts and includes both Taolu (forms) and Sanda (sparring).

Shaolin Kung Fu, also called Shaolin Wushu, is one of the oldest, largest, and most famous styles of wushu, or martial arts of China. It combines Zen Buddhism and martial arts, originating in the Shaolin Temple in Henan province.

The Horse Stance (Mabu) is a fundamental posture in many Asian martial arts. Taking the form of a person riding a horse, it is an essential training method for developing leg strength, balance, and deep rooting into the ground.

Dantian loosely translates as 'elixir field' or 'sea of qi'. It refers to the vital energy centers in the body in traditional Chinese medicine and martial arts. The lower Dantian, located slightly below the navel, is considered the physical center of gravity and the seat of internal energy.

Yin and Yang is a philosophical concept describing how seemingly opposite or contrary forces may actually be complementary, interconnected, and interdependent in the natural world. In martial arts, this manifests as hard/soft, active/passive, and tension/relaxation.

Prana is the Sanskrit word for 'life force' or 'vital principle'. Similar to Chi in Chinese philosophy, it is the energy that permeates reality on all levels, including inanimate objects. Cultivating Prana is a key focus in Yogic practices and holistic health.

Holistic health is an approach to life that considers multidimensional aspects of wellness. It encourages individuals to recognize the whole person: physical, mental, emotional, social, intellectual, and spiritual, rather than just treating symptoms.

Sanda (or Sanshou) is the official Chinese martial art full-contact sport. It is a fighting system developed by the Chinese military based upon the study and practices of traditional Kung Fu and modern combat fighting techniques.

Taolu refers to the set routine (form) practice component of Wushu. Taolu routines comprise a continuously connected set of predetermined techniques, choreographed according to certain principles and philosophies.

Kung Fu (or Gung Fu) is a Chinese term referring to any study, learning, or practice that requires patience, energy, and time to complete. In the West, it is commonly used to describe Chinese martial arts.
A Dojo is a hall or place for immersive learning or meditation. In Japanese, it literally translates to 'Place of the Way'. It is traditionally used in the context of martial arts training.
Sifu (or Shifu) is a title for, and the role of, a skillful person or a master. In the context of martial arts, it translates to 'master' or 'teacher' and implies a fatherly or mentor role.
Kihon is a Japanese term meaning 'basics' or 'fundamentals'. The term is used to refer to the basic techniques that are taught and practiced as the foundation of most Japanese martial arts.
Kata is a Japanese word meaning literally 'form', referring to a detailed choreographed pattern of martial arts movements made to be practiced alone or in groups.

Kumite literally translates to 'grappling hands' and is one of the three main sections of karate training, along with kata and kihon. Kumite is the part of karate in which a person trains against an adversary, using the techniques learned from the kihon and kata.

Self-defense is a countermeasure that involves defending the health and well-being of oneself from harm. Martial arts programs specifically tailor self-defense training to deal with real-world scenarios, teaching awareness, de-escalation, and physical techniques.

Flexibility is the range of motion in a joint or group of joints. In martial arts, high flexibility is crucial for preventing injuries, improving posture, and executing high kicks and deep stances effectively.

Agility is the ability to move quickly and change direction with ease. It involves speed, balance, reflexes, and coordination. Martial artists develop agility to evade attacks and swiftly counter-strike.

Chakras are various focal points used in a variety of ancient meditation practices. While originally from Indian traditions, the concept of energy centers aligns closely with the Dantian concept in Chinese energy cultivation.

Meditation is a practice where an individual uses a technique to train attention and awareness, and achieve a mentally clear and emotionally calm and stable state. It is a critical component of internal martial arts like Tai Chi and Qigong.

In martial arts, a form (or pattern) is a series of choreographed movements, combining strikes, blocks, and stances. Practicing forms helps students build muscle memory, perfect technique, and cultivate internal energy.

Sparring is a form of training common to many combat sports and martial arts. Although the precise form varies, it is essentially 'free-form' fighting under controlled conditions and rules to practice techniques dynamically.

Hypertrophy is the increase in the volume of an organ or tissue due to the enlargement of its component cells. In fitness and martial arts conditioning, muscular hypertrophy is targeted to increase physical strength and power output.

Calisthenics are a form of strength training consisting of a variety of movements that exercise large muscle groups using minimal equipment. They are a staple of martial arts conditioning for building functional, bodyweight strength.

Plyometrics, also known as jump training, are exercises in which muscles exert maximum force in short intervals of time. They are used in martial arts to increase explosive power for strikes and takedowns.

Biomechanics is the study of the structure, function, and motion of the mechanical aspects of biological systems. Understanding biomechanics allows martial artists to maximize the efficiency and power of their techniques while preventing injury.

In traditional Chinese medicine and martial arts, Meridians are pathways in the body along which vital energy (Chi) flows. Acupressure points, often targeted in martial arts applications, are located along these meridians.

Acupressure is an alternative medicine technique similar in principle to acupuncture. It is based on the concept of life energy which flows through 'meridians' in the body. In martial arts, pressure points are targeted to cause pain or disrupt energy flow in an opponent.

Zen is a school of Mahayana Buddhism that emphasizes the value of meditation and intuition. The philosophy of Zen is deeply intertwined with martial arts (like Shaolin Kung Fu), emphasizing a calm, empty, and hyper-aware mind (Mushin).

Mushin (meaning 'no mind') is a mental state into which very highly trained martial artists are said to enter during combat. They act and react without conscious thought, relying entirely on intuition and trained reflexes.

Bunkai translates literally as 'analysis' or 'disassembly'. In karate, it refers to the application of fighting techniques extracted from the moves of a 'kata' (form). It bridges the gap between solo practice and practical self-defense.

Wing Chun is a traditional Southern Chinese Kung Fu style and a form of self-defense that requires quick arm movements and strong legs to defeat opponents. It is famous for its close-range combat effectiveness and the 'One-Inch Punch'.

Jeet Kune Do (JKD) is a hybrid martial arts system and life philosophy founded by martial artist Bruce Lee. It is often referred to as a 'style without style' and emphasizes practicality, flexibility, speed, and efficiency.

Rooting is a foundational principle in internal martial arts. It describes the physical and energetic connection a practitioner establishes with the ground, allowing them to absorb incoming force and generate immense striking power.

Fajin is a Chinese martial arts term that refers to the explosive release of internal power (Chi). It involves the sudden, relaxed, and coordinated snapping of the entire body to deliver devastating force.

Neigong (Internal Skill) refers to any of a set of Chinese breathing, meditation and spiritual practice disciplines associated with Daoism and especially the Chinese martial arts. It is the internal foundation that powers external martial techniques.

Qi Deviation (or Qigong Psychosis) refers to a traditional Chinese medical condition thought to be caused by improperly practicing Qigong or internal alchemy. It highlights the importance of learning under a qualified master.

Iron Shirt is a form of hard Qigong where the practitioner trains the body to withstand heavy blows and strikes without sustaining injury. It involves deep breathing, physical conditioning, and energy packing.

Pushing Hands (Tui Shou) is a two-person training routine practiced in internal Chinese martial arts such as Tai Chi. It teaches leverage, timing, sensitivity, and the principle of yielding to overcome force.

Kiai is a Japanese term used in martial arts for the short shout uttered when performing an attacking move. It is intended to focus energy, regulate breathing, and psychologically disrupt the opponent.

Dim Mak (Death Touch) is an ancient martial arts technique involving striking specific pressure points on the opponent's body to cause incapacitation or severe injury. It requires a profound understanding of anatomy and meridians.
Grappling is a hand-to-hand combat technique that relies on grabbing, holding, and taking down an opponent without striking. It includes throws, sweeps, joint locks, and submission holds.

Striking in martial arts refers to hitting an opponent with a part of the body, such as the fists, elbows, knees, or feet. Styles like Boxing, Muay Thai, and Sanda heavily emphasize striking techniques.

Cardiovascular endurance is the ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to deliver oxygen to working muscles and tissues. It is essential in martial arts for sustaining high-intensity effort during training and sparring.

Mindfulness is the psychological process of purposely bringing one's attention to experiences occurring in the present moment without judgment. It is cultivated through meditation and is key to achieving focus in martial arts.

Weapons training (Kobudo in Japanese or Bingqi in Chinese) involves the practice of traditional martial arts weapons such as the staff (Bo/Gun), sword (Katana/Jian), or nunchaku. It extends the practitioner's reach and enhances coordination.

The 18 Hands of Lohan (Luohan Shi Ba Shou) is a legendary foundational set of exercises developed by Bodhidharma at the Shaolin Temple. It combines yoga-like stretching, deep breathing, and martial applications to unify mind, body, and spirit.
